content:
2 min read
Just now

--

These are two opposite ends — you just make a text file, and the zine asdf + common lisp hoovers up the class / application frame.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

A non-wizard user submits a text file for the zine. It has to be a lisp project, but you can just directly clone https://codeberg.org/screwlisp/simple-text-clim-frame . Technically, I guess you need McCLIM already ( https://codeberg.org/McCLIM/McCLIM ) to run it, but really the only file you need to do is the some.text file.
I am not going to belabor the point further. Just fork the repo, ideally rename it slightly (everywhere), and write your own text instead of submitting my frankly great example text.
The :simple-text-clim-frame/frame frame.lisp is what gets hoovered up by my zine maker. Confusingly, I did not add the command table to let you switch between zine pages here, because that is not something for you to worry about. If you can run lisp, just put your project at the top of the zine’s imports e.g. in :zine/issue/0/all before issuing (asdf:load-system :zine/issue/0/run) (in-package :zine/issue/0/run) (eval *zine*) (find-application-frame 'my-zine).
Press enter or click to view image in full size

The zine repo (which lisp users would like to run) is https://codeberg.org/screwlisp/zine .
Well, you can see the screenshot from that end as well.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

Questions comments, here, submissions in the jam community forum https://itch.io/jam/zine and on the mastodon please https://gamerplus.org/@screwlisp . Submissions are definitely still open both for the issue 0 next week, and issue 1 the week after that — make it clear which you are gunning for please.
